title: Senra Systems: Harnessing Human Skill
gist: Senra Systems, a SpaceX-alumni-founded wire harness manufacturer, is attacking skilled assembly (the hardest-to-automate manufacturing bottleneck) by getting 'configuration' right before automation—standardizing tribal knowledge into software work instructions to make technicians 3x more productive at 99% first-pass yield. Backed by a $65M Series B, it targets the $25-50B A&D harness market with ambitions across the $200B total harness market and all of skilled assembly.

themes.theme: Market size, demand drivers, and urgency

insights.claim: US annual spend on wire harnessing for aerospace and defense alone is roughly $25-50 billion, and the total wire harness market is around $200 billion.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Defines a large, addressable market for a company that currently owns a tiny share, framing the upside if Senra captures scale.
insights.quote: In the US alone, annual spend on wire harnessing for just aerospace and defense is something like $25-50 billion.
insights.locator: In the US alone, annual spend

insights.claim: US orbital launch cadence is accelerating sharply, with 3,708 objects sent into space in 2025 versus 2,261 in 2024, and those numbers are expected to look tiny within a decade.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Rising launch volume directly scales demand for custom harnesses, supporting Senra's demand-side thesis.
insights.quote: America sent 3,708 objects into space in 2025, up from 2,261 in 2024, and those numbers should look tiny in a decade.
insights.locator: America sent 3,708 objects

insights.claim: The FY2026 US national defense budget request crossed $1 trillion for the first time, including $153.3 billion for procurement and $142 billion for research & development.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: A record defense budget skewed toward hardware procurement translates to structural demand growth for harnesses.
insights.quote: the fiscal year 2026 national defense budget request crossed $1 trillion for the first time, including $153.3 billion for procurement and $142 billion for research & development.
insights.locator: Meanwhile, the fiscal year 2026

insights.claim: Electronics content in defense airframes is rising fast: 35% of the F-35's cost, and projected to exceed 40% of the ~$300 million F-47 airframe by the 2030s—meaning more harnesses per platform.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Electrification of weapons systems compounds per-unit harness demand on top of unit-volume growth, a double tailwind.
insights.quote: Electronics comprise 35% of the cost of the F35 Lightning II, more than the cost of the engine itself
insights.locator: As we wrote in Electromagnetism

insights.claim: The AI data center buildout and broad electrification of everything will require substantially more wiring across many industries, expanding harness demand well beyond aerospace and defense.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Positions harnesses as a picks-and-shovels play on two of the largest secular capex cycles (AI infra and electrification).
insights.quote: Both the AI data center buildout and the electrification of everything means that there's going to be a lot more wiring everywhere.
insights.locator: In the past month alone

insights.claim: The problem is characterized as urgent and latent: skilled-assembly shortages will not be acutely felt today but will bite within the next two to five years as the workforce retires into surging demand.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Frames a two-to-five-year window where first movers who solve configuration can capture share before the crunch peaks.
insights.quote: It's a really fucking big problem waiting to happen; you can't necessarily feel it today, but you'll feel it in the next two to five years.
insights.locator: We think that skilled assembly

themes.theme: Why harnesses are the bottleneck (technical and structural)

insights.claim: Wire harnesses are the last-designed but first-needed part of any modern aerospace/defense system, because their routing can't be finalized until the whole machine's architecture is set, yet nothing turns on without them.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: This sequencing makes harnesses a critical-path bottleneck, giving a fast, reliable supplier outsized leverage over customer timelines.
insights.quote: The wire harness is one of the last designed but first needed parts of any modern aerospace and defense system.
insights.locator: Because the wire harness supply chain

insights.claim: Harnesses are devilishly hard to automate because they are high-mix, low-volume, 3D, bendy, and floppy—one satellite company inside a Prime builds around 8,000 unique harnesses per year.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: The physical intractability of automation is precisely why the human-centric 'configuration' approach can win where robotics can't yet.
insights.quote: One satellite company inside of one Prime builds something like 8,000 unique wire harnesses every year.
insights.locator: The fun part, and the part

insights.claim: Faulty wire harnesses cause catastrophic failures and recalls, historically implicated in the fatal 1967 Apollo 1 fire and, on June 9, in Jeep's recall of over 1 million Wranglers and Gladiators for a power-steering-pump harness fault.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Failure cost is life-and-recall severe, so customers pay premiums for quality and reliability—Senra's core selling point.
insights.quote: It was a bad wire harness that took the lives of those three astronauts.
insights.locator: As the three men sat

insights.claim: The wire harness supply chain is broken end-to-end: designs are still done in Excel spreadsheets and PowerPoint slides, and manufacturing is done entirely by hand, 'arts and crafts style.'
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: Antiquated tooling signals large low-hanging-fruit improvements available to a software-native vertically integrated entrant.
insights.quote: It's a really screwed up supply chain from start to finish.
insights.locator: A wire harness is in everything

insights.claim: Legacy harness shops quote four-to-six-month lead times on new programs, and only 25-50% of harnesses pass on the first try, forcing customers to employ in-house technicians just to fix supplier defects.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Poor incumbent NPS, slow lead times, and low first-pass yield are the exact quality/speed gaps Senra monetizes.
insights.quote: lead times on a new program are 4-6 months, only 25-50% of wire harnesses pass on the first try (first-pass yield)
insights.locator: This supply/demand imbalance

insights.claim: Harnesses can be extremely custom and complex—some designs require a single cable with 45 different offshoots all built to spec—which makes sourcing far harder than the simple 'plug this into that' framing implies.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: High customization justifies the design-feedback and engineering-services layer Senra sells alongside manufacturing.
insights.quote: you need a cable that has 45 different offshoots, and they all need to be built to spec, so they're highly custom products.
insights.locator: Sourcing high-quality harnesses

insights.claim: The closest public workforce category to wire harness technician (electrical/electronic/electromechanical assemblers) has a median age of 47.2, with over a quarter aged 55+ and only 8% under 25.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: An aging, un-backfilled workforce is a structural supply constraint that a faster training pipeline directly arbitrages.
insights.quote: the closest public workforce category - electrical, electronic, and electromechanical assemblers - has a median age of 47.2.
insights.locator: There is no government statistical

insights.claim: Traditional harness training runs an 18-month apprenticeship built on shadowing a single veteran ('follow Doug around'), so retiring workers take irreplaceable tribal knowledge with them because they never write it down.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: The 18-month training bottleneck caps how fast incumbents can scale—Senra's four-week program is the wedge to break it.
insights.quote: There's this guy Doug, he's been building Boeing harnesses for 25 years. Just go follow him around and see if you can learn something.
insights.locator: Relying on veterans' head-knowledge

insights.claim: The US harness supply base is roughly 800 small, owner-operated shops whose growth is capped by their limited number of veterans available to train apprentices.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: A fragmented, capacity-constrained supply base is ripe for a scaled, vertically integrated roll-up-style consolidator.
insights.quote: The roughly 800 American harness shops that make up the supply base are largely small, owner-operated businesses.
insights.locator: The roughly 800 American harness

insights.claim: Government data corroborates the crunch: a 2024 GAO review found 15 of 17 commercial aviation manufacturers struggled to hire enough skilled workers, and the IPC trade association calls the skilled-worker shortage 'chronic.'
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Third-party government and trade validation de-risks the demand thesis beyond the founder's own framing.
insights.quote: the Government Accountability Office (GAO) found that 15 of the 17 manufacturers it interviewed had struggled to hire enough skilled workers to meet demand.
insights.locator: The government knows its suppliers

insights.claim: A&D harness work can neither be automated nor outsourced because it is ITAR-protected, effectively requiring American-sourced production and concentrating the workforce shortage there.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: ITAR creates a regulatory moat that shields domestic harness makers from offshore competition, protecting margins.
insights.quote: it's ITAR-protected, meaning that customers are effectively required to source American.
insights.locator: A&D is the highest-margin segment

insights.claim: Senra's central thesis is that you can't automate until you can configure, and that configuration—orchestrating humans, machines, software, materials, and processes—is the layer above automation that counts automation as merely one of its tools.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: This reframes the investment away from robotics hype toward a systems/software play, differentiating Senra from lights-out automation bets.
insights.quote: You can't automate until you can configure.
insights.locator: And it turns out you can't automate

insights.claim: Senra follows an explicit Standardize -> Optimize -> Automate sequence, echoing Elon Musk's five-step design algorithm where automation comes last.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: A disciplined sequencing gives a repeatable playbook to generalize across product lines, not a one-off manufacturing improvement.
insights.quote: The way we see it, you need to standardize, then optimize, then automate, in that order.
insights.locator: Standardize → Optimize → Automate

insights.claim: Standardization means converting a customer's harness drawing into detailed step-by-step software work instructions; today this conversion is only about 20% automated, done by a team of engineers.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: The current 20% automation rate quantifies the headroom—and the data flywheel—available as models take over drawing-to-instructions.
insights.quote: Right now, the process is like 20% automated, which is fine, because even having work instructions is a step-up.
insights.locator: Today, Senra takes in a wire harness

insights.claim: Senra expects to use its human-crafted work instructions to train models that get roughly 90% of the way from drawing to work instructions, with an engineer doing the final check-off.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: The proprietary work-instruction dataset is the moat input for an AI optimization step competitors can't easily replicate.
insights.quote: it can use those human-crafted work instructions to train models that can get to somewhere like 90% of the way from drawing to work instructions
insights.locator: Over time, it can use those

insights.claim: Senra is building a digital twin of the manufacturing process, digitizing data at every point and then building custom machines (e.g., barcode-driven cut-and-strip) that integrate with its software as tools for human technicians.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: Owning the full digital thread plus custom tooling creates data and process advantages that deepen with volume.
insights.quote: We are basically creating a digital twin of the manufacturing process, capturing and digitizing data at every point so that we can optimize each and every part.
insights.locator: We are basically creating a digital

insights.claim: Automation is being introduced selectively by dexterity: cutting/stripping is easy to machine, crimping is next, but high-dexterity connector insertion probably won't be automated for a very long time.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: A realistic automation roadmap tempers over-promising and reinforces the durable value of skilled human technicians.
insights.quote: Something like connector insertion is such a high-dexterity operation that we probably won't automate it for a very long time.
insights.locator: Crimping – taking a piece of metal

insights.claim: Senra prioritizes making technicians 3x more productive than industry average by optimizing everything around them over pursuing full lights-out automation.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: The explicit deprioritization of full automation is the contrarian bet an investor is underwriting.
insights.quote: We care a lot more about that than we do about full lights-out automation.
insights.locator: We think we can make our technicians

themes.theme: Operating metrics, unit economics, and Amp platform

insights.claim: Senra hires people who have never touched a wire harness and, after a four-week apprentice program, has them building harnesses on the production floor for customers including SpaceX and Anduril.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: Compressing 18 months of training to four weeks is the core scalability unlock and a direct labor-cost advantage.
insights.quote: Senra hires people who have never touched a wire harness, and after the four-week program, they're building harnesses on the production floor for SpaceX and Anduril.
insights.locator: Senra hires people who have never

insights.claim: Senra reports a 99% first-pass yield versus roughly 75% for competitors, and claims it can deliver in four weeks a better product than incumbents turn around in four months.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: A 99% vs 75% yield gap plus 4-week vs 4-month turnaround is the quantified performance edge underpinning pricing power.
insights.quote: we're at a 99% first-pass yield, versus 75% for our competitors.
insights.locator: Meanwhile, again because we can track

insights.claim: Senra can bid jobs at a 20% margin to win business, expecting to optimize the process to well north of 50% margin over the life of a contract.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: The 20%-to-50%+ margin expansion path is the unit-economics thesis: win on price, then earn on process improvement.
insights.quote: we can bid on jobs at a 20% margin to win the business with the comfort that we'll be able to optimize the process to well north of 50% over the life of the contract.
insights.locator: We can deliver in four weeks

insights.claim: Amp is Senra's operating system—modeled on SpaceX's WarpDrive—spanning design, quoting/BOM (AI reads messy RFQs), procurement/kitting, manufacturing planning, build with a 100% digital thread, and real-time quality enforcement.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: A single vertically integrated software backbone is what enables generalization to other products and the data moat.
insights.quote: our Amp platform, which is like our operating system, Senra's version of SpaceX's WarpDrive.
insights.locator: And we're tracking and collecting data

insights.claim: Individual Senra customers are each doing hundreds of millions of dollars a year in wire harnesses and are impatient for Senra to expand capacity to take on more of that business.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: Demand-pull from very large existing accounts means growth is capacity-constrained, not demand-constrained—a favorable posture.
insights.quote: individual customers of ours are doing hundreds of millions of dollars a year in wire harnesses, and they are impatient for us to expand capacity
insights.locator: For a sense of scale

insights.claim: Senra's attrition is 'comically low,' which the company attributes partly to basic workplace quality like offering air conditioning and a pleasant environment.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: Low attrition protects the training investment and compounds the skilled-workforce bench that is core to the moat.
insights.quote: our attrition is comically low because we offer air conditioning and a pleasant workplace
insights.locator: Far before automating skilled assembly

themes.theme: Moat, business model, and competitive dynamics

insights.claim: Senra's near-term moat is simply that the work is really hard to do well, but at scale it intends to build durable 7 Powers moats in Scale Economies and Process Power.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: Names the specific durable-advantage mechanisms an investor should track maturing as the company scales.
insights.quote: as we scale, we dig traditional 7 Powers moats in the form of Scale Economies and Process Power.
insights.locator: For now, the fact that this is really

insights.claim: Senra fits Keith Rabois's startup-success formula—find a large, highly fragmented, low-NPS industry and vertically integrate to simplify the value product.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Maps Senra onto a recognized venture pattern, signaling why sophisticated investors piled into the round.
insights.quote: Find large highly fragmented industry w low NPS; vertically integrate a solution to simplify value product.
insights.locator: Keith Rabois

insights.claim: Senra aims to become the default vendor for all of a customer's harness needs by contracting to provide design and engineering feedback on harnesses even before the manufacturing stage.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: Embedding at the design stage creates switching costs and account control ahead of any manufacturing order.
insights.quote: we're on contract with our customers to provide design and engineering feedback on their harnesses, even before they get to the manufacturing stage.
insights.locator: The practical way we do this

insights.claim: The strategic playbook mirrors peers Hadrian (precision machining) and Poetic—turning tribal knowledge into scalable software rather than 'magically throwing AI at a problem.'
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Situates Senra within an emerging category of software-defined American manufacturing startups worth tracking as a cohort.
insights.quote: turning tribal knowledge into scalable software and processes.
insights.locator: In a traditional shop, practically everything

insights.claim: The founding insight was a Not Boring principle that a company which is a bad customer or supplier can become a very good competitor—Jordan Black left SpaceX to build the harness supplier he couldn't source from.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Founder-market fit rooted in acute personal pain often predicts sharper product and customer empathy.
insights.quote: if a company is a bad customer or supplier, they might make a very good competitor.
insights.locator: A longstanding theme of Not Boring

themes.theme: Expansion beyond A&D and long-term vision

insights.claim: Senra started in A&D because it is the highest-margin, most fragmented, highest-willingness-to-pay, and least-automatable slice, but intends to own as much of the $200 billion total harness market as possible.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: Frames A&D as a beachhead, not the ceiling—material to how large the TAM narrative can grow.
insights.quote: we want to own as much of the $200 billion total wire harness market as we can eventually.
insights.locator: It's a matter of when, not if

insights.claim: Inbound is already broadening: in the past month Senra was approached by companies making data-center chillers and USPS mail trucks, with automotive representing low-mix high-volume work (e.g., an electric truck company runs just 25 harness types repeated on every truck).
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: Early adjacency pull validates generalizability, though low-mix work builds less of the skilled bench that differentiates A&D.
insights.quote: we've been hit up by companies making chillers for data centers and mail trucks for the US Postal Service, and a lot in between.
insights.locator: But we want to fix all of skilled

insights.claim: Amp was deliberately built to generalize as a design-and-manufacturing platform (not a harness-only tool), so processes and software can be copy-pasted into adjacent skilled-assembly work like sensors and electromechanical assembly.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: Platform generality is the mechanism that could turn a harness company into a broad skilled-assembly platform—the real bull case.
insights.quote: We built Amp to generalize; it's a design and manufacturing software platform, not a wire harness-only tool.
insights.locator: As we figure out how to standardize

insights.claim: The ultimate vision is making skilled-assembly products available Amazon-style: a customer designs a harness or sensor on Senra's website, clicks buy, and receives it two weeks later.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: An e-commerce-for-hardware endgame implies a much larger, higher-multiple software-plus-manufacturing business if achieved.
insights.quote: You should be able to go to our website, design your harness or your sensor or whatever you need, click buy, and get it two weeks later.
insights.locator: Wire harness manufacturing is really a wedge

themes.theme: Team, financing, and the human-skill thesis

insights.claim: Senra raised a $65M Series B led by Lowercarbon and Interlagos, with an unusually deep investor syndicate including Sequoia, Founders Fund, General Catalyst, Andreessen Horowitz, Dylan Field, 8VC, The Friedkin Group, JAWS, Sozo Ventures, and others.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: A top-tier, oversubscribed-looking syndicate signals strong smart-money conviction and easier future access to capital.
insights.quote: Senra's $65M Series B from Lowercarbon & Interlagos with Sequoia, Founders Fund, General Catalyst, Andreessen Horowitz
insights.locator: So to celebrate Senra's $65M Series B

insights.claim: Senra was founded in March 2023 by SpaceX avionics engineer Jordan Black and Ben Shanahan, a neuroscientist-turned-SpaceX software engineer who built the WarpDrive ERP/MES.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Founders who built and depended on SpaceX's manufacturing OS bring rare domain-plus-software credibility to a hardware problem.
insights.quote: In March 2023, they founded Senra Systems.
insights.locator: So Jordan decided to leave SpaceX

insights.claim: Senra recently recruited two senior ex-SpaceX leaders: Ken Venner, SpaceX's former CIO and WarpDrive architect, as Chief Technology and Product Officer, and Jessica Chavarria, an eight-year SpaceX supply-chain veteran, as Head of Supply Chain.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: Attracting the actual WarpDrive architect to build Amp is strong talent validation and de-risks the software execution.
insights.quote: This is the most excited I've been about a startup since SpaceX.
insights.locator: Ken Venner was the CIO at SpaceX

insights.claim: A recurring signal is that financially independent SpaceX 'grinders' who could earn hundreds of millions and retire are instead choosing to work at Senra, treating it as validation that solving skilled assembly is among the most important things they could do.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: Talent that self-selects into a hard problem it deeply understands is a soft but meaningful signal of opportunity size.
insights.quote: the people who've been at SpaceX long enough to know where all the bodies are buried are saying “If we could solve this, this is the most important thing we could work on.”
insights.locator: We've created a place where the grinders

insights.claim: The core worldview is that as easy work gets automated, skilled humans become more valuable—citing Travis Kalanick's prediction that skilled workers will get paid like LeBron James because they are the long pole in the tent to accelerating progress.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: This contrarian human-augmentation thesis (vs full-automation orthodoxy) is the philosophical bet distinguishing Senra from robotics plays.
insights.quote: the more the easy stuff gets automated, the more valuable skilled humans will become.
insights.locator: Ours is a belief that the more

insights.claim: Senra frames robotics adoption in manufacturing as future-constrained less by hardware capability than by the absence of structured, well-configured operational environments—so it is encoding manufacturing into machine-readable systems now to be ready.
insights.speaker: Jordan Black
insights.so_what: If correct, Senra's configured data environments become the substrate that eventual robots plug into—an option on the robotics wave without betting on its timing.
insights.quote: we believe that robotics adoption in manufacturing will be constrained less by hardware and more by the absence of structured, well-configured operational environments.
insights.locator: When the time comes
